podcasts.name: No Priors
podcasts.title: Travel Through the Lens of AI with with Booking.com CEO Glenn Fogel
podcasts.transcript:
Speaker 1 | 00:00 - 00:13
There is no such thing as a moat. There is no such thing as somewhere you're gonna be protected against innovation. Today, we have a competitive advantage on areas. Absolutely. But those can go away tomorrow.

Speaker 1 | 00:13 - 00:24
The only one to win long term is to continue to develop new services, new ways to do things. How can we do your business better? What do you need? Where you need more demand? Where are you hurting?

Speaker 1 | 00:24 - 00:36
It's so much more complex. Complex. If you think you're just gonna come in and do this business and knock away these very big players, I'd say you should really understand what the business is before you decide to commit your capital.

Speaker 2 | 00:43 - 01:16
Today on our priors, we're talking with Glenn Fogel, the CEO of Booking Holdings, a twenty something year veteran of the company who joined when it was just Priceline and worth a few $100,000,000 in the year 2000 and has since helped grow it to a $100,000,000,000 plus company with multiple massive assets across travel that are now being used in the AI era. We will talk about a variety of topics with Glenn that span everything from, the growth and changes that have happened in bookings over time, as well as his own career and perspective on AI. So, Glenn, thank you so much for joining us today.

Speaker 1 | 01:16 - 01:18
Well, thank you very much for having me.

Speaker 2 | 01:18 - 01:37
Yeah. So you've had a really interesting career. You know, you went to Wharton, you went to Harvard Law. Along the way, you worked in, I think, the MIS program at Morgan Stanley. I'd love to just hear a little bit about your early background and how that led you eventually to booking.com and eventually Booking Holdings.

Speaker 1 | 01:37 - 02:00
Sure. I can do that. So real short, I came out of work with Green Finance, and now I end up at the back office in MIS. My first job is putting tapes on a drive in a data center of IBM thirty eighty fours and the discs that went and and the tape drives and put the put the tapes on on the drive. That's where I started.

Speaker 1 | 02:00 - 02:15
There's an operator. Actually, being an operator of a mainframe, very different. Nothing that you prepared for that in college for that. And I then become you become a developer, and you but I basically learned that this is not a career for me. I should do something else.

Speaker 1 | 02:15 - 02:41
And all the people I knew, undergrad and were and were going off to investment bank and made all this money, and I thought, fuck. Try to do that, but you can't go. I've now gone down one shoot, and you can't just jump out of that one to go become an investment banker. So I said, oh, I I agree to finance warrant, Harvard let me into law school, so I'll do that because that's another route. I did that, and I end up getting a job on Wall Street as a banker.

Speaker 1 | 02:41 - 03:04
I did that for till '95 till 1995. And then the bank was bought by another bank, and they fired all the bankers almost, not every bank. Fired most, including me, but not everybody because they fired everybody because they well, they fired everybody. But they didn't fire everybody. So you there was a there was actually some people were picked to stay.

Speaker 1 | 03:05 - 03:31
That was not one of them. That that's pretty bad. So a real good lesson, though, having been fired and knowing what it's like, that is something that I've kept with me throughout my career about how to do it right and how to do it wrong and understand what goes through the other person when you tell them that I'm sorry, but there's no longer a room for you here and this is not the place for you to be. And I I really learned that firsthand being on that side of the table. And so now I'm unemployed.

Speaker 1 | 03:31 - 03:50
And my father had just died, not that long before this happened. My father's died, lost a job. My grandmother died, and he and the dog died, which was kind of sad. And I'm like, what do want to do with my life now? My young thirties, I'm alone, I'm saying no, whatever.

Speaker 1 | 03:50 - 04:03
And I said, you know, I always wanted to write a book. So I start writing a book, I write a novel, I get it done, and now I'm going try and get it published. And it's not really self publishing, man. You weren't doing it on your own. You're trying to get some agent to pick it up.

Speaker 1 | 04:03 - 04:19
And I was introduced to a woman as a a blind date girlfriend. And I think she was a lawyer, but I'm sorry. I don't know. They said, well, she used to work at Random House as an editor. I said, oh, I'm very interested in how you did the And so we have a date, and the book never gets published.

Speaker 1 | 04:19 - 04:35
But I do end up marrying her and have two great kids and has wonderful life. But while I was trying to get the book, you know, an agent to be interested in it, eventually, she said, you know, if this relationship's gonna go forward, you should get a job. I said, damn. Boy, I should. I didn't wanna go back to banking.

Speaker 1 | 04:35 - 04:46
And I said, I don't know. And a friend of mine from law school was a senior type person at Morgan Stanley. And he I told him, look. Amy says I have to get a job. How do thought?

Speaker 1 | 04:46 - 04:54
She said, well, we have this trading position here that you could do. I never traded anything in my life. He said, don't worry. You'll be fine. It's like, okay.

Speaker 1 | 04:54 - 05:09
So I end up being head trader for a guy named Barton Bay. He's kind of a Wall Street legend and stuff. I do that for a number of years, but I just don't like it. It's not that ex it's just not for me. And that's when the Internet was really taking off that first real explosion.

Speaker 1 | 05:09 - 05:33
You know, the Internet boom. That's late nineties. So 1999, I started trying to interview, I think, well, I got some skills. But when I was an IT person, I got that. And I know a little bit about corporate development because being a trusted banker, and I started interviewing the only real company on the East Coast at the time of Internet, you know, abilities was Priceline.

Speaker 1 | 05:33 - 05:53
And they had a job for corporate development. It was perfect. So I get an offer from them, but I said, wanna wait. I wanna wait till I get my bonus for 1999 when it gets paid at the February 2000. So I go and I get my bonus check out, ready to start, And that's when, of course, that's when the Nasdaq peaked.

Speaker 1 | 05:53 - 06:19
Thereby having gotten long Internet a week before when the Nasdaq peaked and stopped. And it it proved that I shouldn't be a trader having just Yeah. The wrong way. And, know, Priceline has its difficulties after that. Our stock went from where we were when we went public, we in a week or so, we were 30,000,000,000, which back then was real money and that meant something back then.

Speaker 1 | 06:19 - 06:36
And by the time I joined I joined after the I you know, few months after the IPO, when I joined, we're probably down about 15,000,000,000. That's now, you know, February, March of 2,000. In nine months, our market cap is now down to just a couple of 100,000,000.

Speaker 2 | 06:36 - 06:37
Wow.

Speaker 1 | 06:37 - 06:49
And our stock is now trading at a dollar a share. We're gonna get delisted because it's dropping below a dollar a share. But stick with it. We do a reverse split to make sure we don't get delisted. So it goes to $6 a share.

Speaker 1 | 06:49 - 07:07
I stay, and I've been there twenty seven on my twenty seventh year. Wow. And we went from that that now reverse split $6. And in last summer, we came very close to $6,000 So over that Yeah. You know, a little more than a quarter of a century, up a thousand times.

Speaker 1 | 07:08 - 07:13
And, you know, market cap was peaking around 180,000,000,000. Remember, it's a few 100,000,000.

Speaker 2 | 07:13 - 07:15
Yeah. It's amazing.

Speaker 1 | 07:15 - 07:26
It's been a good ride so far, but, of course, you never stop. Every day is a new adventure. Every day is you gotta a big headline in the Feet when I had an interview with them, I said, gotta fight for a customer every day.

Speaker 2 | 07:27 - 07:36
How do you think about this the lessons from that Internet era in terms of the current AI wave? Because we're seeing this massive shift in market cap.

Speaker 1 | 07:37 - 07:58
You think like this is a an explosion of new things, so all these would cover everything just like in the late nineties. The optimism of technologies. I thought everything was gonna be wonderful. But then you get a little bit of the backlash coming backlash then too. Now you I guess it's just it's just bigger than it was then.

Speaker 1 | 07:58 - 08:07
The numbers are much bigger. The issues at hand are much bigger, the pluses and the minuses. So but I do see a lot of parallels.

Speaker 2 | 08:07 - 08:37
Yeah. Because, you know, when I look at it right now, some companies clearly have enormous revenue bases. You know, OpenAI and Anthropic are rumored to have 30,000,000,000 to $50,000,000,000 in revenue run rate each. And, in parallel, see companies that are extremely highly valued, 10,000,000,000, etcetera, that don't necessarily have even revenue yet. And if you look at the Internet era, I think it was something like four fifty companies went public in 'ninety nine, four fifty went public in the first few months of 2000, maybe 500 went public before that.

Speaker 2 | 08:37 - 08:51
So you had 1,500 companies of which, what, two dozen are left at most? Maybe. The other fourteen eighty are gone. And so do you think the same thing will happen to the AI set of companies? Do you think something different will happen?

Speaker 2 | 08:51 - 08:56
Like and those aren't IPOs, by the way. Those are the very strongest or perceived to be strongest companies. Right?

Speaker 1 | 08:56 - 09:25
Right. Well, I I I wouldn't wanna even guess at what the ratio of success to failures will be this time around versus that time versus any other time when there was incredible boom. And, you know, if we go over the last hundred and fifty years, there always been these kind of springing further back. Yeah. Speculative booms that create tremendous innovation, people coming in just both money and people.

Speaker 1 | 09:26 - 09:37
I mean, California, you know, the forty niners, I mean, everybody's running off to the hills to gold, and it was gonna rain. I'm sure there were a lot of companies selling axes and hammers.

Speaker 2 | 09:37 - 09:40
Yeah. Auto boom, same thing. We've seen

Speaker 1 | 09:40 - 09:54
this Exactly. And how many of So I don't know, but this is not new. And there'll be a great deal of disappointment. There'll be a lot of people who gonna lose a lot of money, of course. That's just the nature of how our economy is working.

Speaker 1 | 09:54 - 10:01
When they're in speculative bubbles, that will bring all up, but that doesn't mean that there aren't a lot of companies. That's a real value it's gonna make.

Speaker 2 | 10:02 - 10:22
How do you think as a founder running a company or as a CEO, you should make the decision in terms of whether to keep going or whether to sell? It's kind of like, okay, Priceline is worth a couple $100,000,000, and the decision was made, we'll keep going no matter what. And there may or may not have been options in terms of exits. I have no idea. But in today's era, there's quite a few options in terms of exits.

Speaker 2 | 10:22 - 10:23
Should people mainly be thinking about exiting

Speaker 1 | 10:23 - 10:24
right now, do you think?

Speaker 2 | 10:24 - 10:26
Do you think they should keep going?

Speaker 1 | 10:26 - 10:40
Yeah. I think that's right. I don't think we can give a general rule without knowing what the facts of that specific situation are. By the way, there were times early in the day where price would have been happy if somebody has made an offer. I mean, look.

Speaker 1 | 10:40 - 10:56
Yeah. Yeah. Offer. It really depends a lot on what the situation is, how confident is management and people who have put the money in that which there is going to be a vulture, and how concerned are you? What are you really trying to do?

Speaker 1 | 10:56 - 11:07
Are trying to accomplish something? The goal is to actually make something that matters, or are you just here to make money? And nothing wrong with that. I'm not against that. So you gotta understand what is your motivation?

Speaker 1 | 11:07 - 11:23
What are you trying to achieve? We have on average I don't know if you're American male, for the hell of a because I think that's what I've looked at. I have a seventy eight year expected lifespan when you're born. Of course, the longer you live, the higher you expect. Whatever it is, and how are you gonna spend those years?

Speaker 1 | 11:23 - 11:33
What is important to you? What do you wanna do? What's the meaning to it? And I'll let the people who are actually involved in those situations. I would not I would not give them any general advice.

Speaker 2 | 11:33 - 12:02
Makes sense. So, I mean, back to booking. You know, one of the categories that you all obviously are really crucial to is travel. And there's a number of the next gen sort of AI companies who've started experimenting with things like OpenAI, had, checkout and ChatGPT, and, you know, one of the main use cases was travel, and then they canceled that feature. And I think at the time, booking went up 8% on the news.

Speaker 2 | 12:02 - 12:08
What what do you think didn't work there? What what do you think how do you think people should think about travel through the lens of AI?

Speaker 1 | 12:08 - 12:49
So I think we should back up a little bit so everybody understands what's going on here. So in any type of situation, you'll have people who are not that knowledgeable about the industry or about how, things actually happen in the business. And so from the outside, it looked rather easy to, oh, this will be easy. AI will take care of travel, and all the travel companies won't be worthwhile, won't be worth anything. And so and that was why companies like ourselves took a big hit as some of the new models were dropped that had a much better way of doing agent in commerce as it was perceived at the time.

Speaker 1 | 12:49 - 13:10
And then when the when people make analysis, oh, we're not gonna do that, like when OpenAI said, we're not planning to be a merchant of record trying to or we're not even gonna keep in this app type of way of doing commerce. We're not doing it. Then people say, oh, well, I was wrong. I'm not gonna worry about it as much. It flips the other side.

Speaker 1 | 13:10 - 13:32
The truth is the way we look at AI is an incredible beneficial tool and a way for us to be able to do our mission easier, hopefully cheaper, and better for our customers. All business. What is the purpose of a business? A business is to do something of value to its customers. We have two kinds of customers.

Speaker 1 | 13:32 - 13:59
We've got travelers and we've got partners. Are in the middle of that for a marketplace. How can we do it better? AI, particularly AI using, large language models and other things like that can help make it a much more valuable method for people, travelers to get information they need to do what they wanna do and beneficial to our partners. That's the thing.

Speaker 1 | 13:59 - 14:08
Now the idea of chat TV, you know, telling me no longer having one method. I mean, I wouldn't read too much into that one way or the other. Mhmm.

Speaker 2 | 14:08 - 14:20
Yeah. That makes sense. You know, it's interesting because I'm in the middle of Silicon Valley where people are very AGI pills. Right? People strongly believe that AI will drive all sorts of things, and in some cases, will.

Speaker 2 | 14:20 - 14:41
And in some cases, it'll take longer. In some cases, it won't. You know, it reminds me a little bit of crypto where crypto was gonna solve everything, and it didn't, but it was very important for certain aspects of the financial system. And I think stablecoins and other things are increasingly viable there. On the AI side, what a lot of people are really moving towards is more agentic work, and that could be specific companies like Decagon having agents to do customer support.

Speaker 2 | 14:41 - 15:22
But it's also the larger platforms like OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, etcetera, providing increasingly self driven systems, codecs or Cloud Cowork or, you know, some of the things Gemini is doing. And one of the arguments people are making is that the nature of UI is going to change, and you're going to have agents doing transactions on your behalf and sourcing things like trips or figuring out your travel itinerary for you or buying or purchasing the actual different aspects of travel. A, do you think that's a correct vision of the world? And, b, do you think or how do you view that interacting with booking and what you all provide as a service?

Speaker 1 | 15:23 - 15:42
So again, we wanna reduce this to understanding what does the customer want. Many people with travel find it very frustrating. I know that. People that will travel find very frustrating. Trying to put together a complicated trip with family, let's say, multiple destinations, different things you wanna do.

It's complex. It's pain. You start planning it, then you stop because it's just too much of a pain. And everybody would like something else. Many people would like something else to do it for them.

Speaker 1 | 15:53 - 16:14
In fact, that's why you'll find very wealthy people have travel concierge people who are actual human beings who really understand the needs of that customer, what they really like and not, and they will do a lot of the hard work for them. People aren't quite in that wealth zone. They'll have there's a partner or their spouse trying to it for them. I'll be perfectly honest. I'm exposing myself here, but I'll say it.

Speaker 1 | 16:14 - 16:29
Okay? My wife and I sometimes argue about, okay. Who's gonna have to do all the travel planning for this trip? Because it can be frustrating, etcetera. Now with AI, the beauty is it's gonna make it so much easier, and it's doing it right now.

Speaker 1 | 16:29 - 16:56
We are doing it right now. And let's use that generic term. Let's call it an agent. So I can't wait until we, Booking Holdings in our companies, are offering up these personalized agents that are they know everything about you, everything you want, and able to do so much more for you than any human travel agent could ever do because the machine never forgets anything. The machine has an infinite amount of permutation.

Speaker 1 | 16:56 - 17:04
It rapidly look through and choose what is the best thing, and it can go down and then back up. That doesn't work. Why? This doesn't fit that well. Can come back with it.

Speaker 1 | 17:04 - 17:26
Now, people always want some agency, so they make the decision themselves or at least confirm they want it. Most people aren't, for the most part, a complicated thing. They're going to want it to double check them, check them, whatever, which is different than say a business person says, gotta go from New York to Chicago. And, you know, your assistant, your human assistant, right now, is doing it for you. That's like an agent doing it for you.

Speaker 1 | 17:26 - 17:35
He knows what flight you need and all that. That's great. Yeah. But when it's complicated, you want that agency. So we at Booking Holdings, all our companies, we are doing that right now.

Speaker 1 | 17:35 - 17:59
In fact, if you go to Penny, which is Priceline's agentic AI system, and I just did it the other night. I want them in the back of the bus. They're starting.

So I got two cabins now. There are one person has to go back to a different city. We're going to Europe. We're going to a city. We're not we're not actually doing the trip from that city where we're gonna land.

Speaker 1 | 18:09 - 18:19
How are gonna get from where we go? Should we have the hotel where we land and then travel the next day to the other city, or should we go that night? How are gonna do it? What restaurant? All the thing.

Speaker 1 | 18:19 - 18:39
And I didn't on Priceline, Penny, and it was incredible. I also added another things. I told it that, by the way, I got a lot of frequent flyer miles. So should I be using my miles or should I use it cash and for which ones? And it was just beautiful how it went back and forth and asked me questions like, how many miles do you have with each airline that I'm giving it?

Speaker 1 | 18:39 - 18:56
And then we're going through the flight part and how much of the cost. By the way, ended up, as I suspected, I'm using my miles for the upfront part for my wife and I. We're paying cash for the kids on our flights. We're going go to the hotel in the city that we landed. The next day, we're going to get a shelf.

Speaker 1 | 18:56 - 19:10
Was wonderful. It was wonderful. And that's what we want even more so. Here's the real important thing is when things go wrong and things go wrong in travel, nobody's fault. Many times, weather, mechanics, it happens.

Speaker 1 | 19:10 - 19:24
Okay? You wanna have that one point of contact that can everything because travel is like dominoes. One thing falls over, and it all starts falling over. And that's the beauty with AI being able to figure out being able to look ahead. What can we do?

Speaker 1 | 19:24 - 19:37
My goal is to have a system that actually we are able to predict well enough what the problem will be before it happens and suggest changing things. I have so many examples of this, but I see the future.

Speaker 2 | 19:37 - 20:14
I guess at a sort of generic level, because your team on a call that we had prior said that Penny adoption, which again is this agentic tool that you've built for Priceline, has doubled every month for the past past few months. And it's generated a lift in conversion plus faster search, shorter path to booking, lower cancellation, higher customer success. So it seems like it's working in really interesting ways. Are there a common set of use cases that you think are most common for Penny? Are there you know, specific things that it doesn't do well that you just need the underlying models to get better for?

I'm a little bit curious though.

It doesn't really show up much in the numbers yet because it's still really, really small in terms of the absolute number. We ain't gotta talk scale here. You know, last year, we did a $186,000,000,000 worth of travel.

That that's a lot of travel. You don't get over a billion room nights. So the actual numbers, so why? Well, part of it is we're not pushing it really fully. There's one of the issues that I always wanna think about is what's the cost of this?

Speaker 1 | 20:51 - 20:59
And that's not so easy to understand. What is the cost? That's what's developing of running it. How many tokens are we consuming? Where?

Where? Where? How many times are they coming back and forth or doing? Tell me how much was the cost of us getting that trip for that person, and what is our ROI going to be? Then the next thing is, well, what's the return long term?

Speaker 1 | 21:13 - 21:18
Did it mean lifetime value? Did they come back? Are the loyalties up or what? How much? And will it change?

Speaker 1 | 21:18 - 21:30
These are things that we don't know yet that we're gonna have to do. We need to work on develop till we know. And by the way, the whole thing of token economics now, which model should we be using? For which purpose? And when?

And stuff. You know, obviously, you can get tokens a lot cheaper and certain parts to different models, and they're a lot cheaper. And that's something that we have to look at very closely too. So it is fascinating that we can do things that I I'm so thrilled that we do. Like, for example, things like using customer service right now.

Speaker 1 | 21:50 - 22:06
Customer service where we're using AI, it's it's great. It happens much faster. You you know, instead of having to staff your humans and at peak time, somebody's got to wait for somebody to pick up. We've all been in that line. In that queue, waiting for someone to pick up, and we hate it.

Speaker 1 | 22:06 - 22:36
But now with AI, the computer can pick it up. It's not a problem. And solve the problem even better and faster, than you never will have in the future. But here's the question again about that is sometimes though, you want to figure out because people want to talk to humans sometimes.

You got to balance that because what you don't want to do is end up, yeah, you can do it all AI, but actually that's not what the customer wants. And in the end, it's always what's best for the customer.

Yeah. That makes sense. And I I think you said on your q one call that customer service costs are already down about 10%. Well, we For reservation and

Let's booking security about 10 just go let's go this just this Our costs per customer service per contact are down. Mhmm. Mhmm. That's great. Customer satisfaction is up.

That's even better. But we have to make sure that we are able to always recognize some customers want a human being and some customers are, you know, you're happy as could be just through AI.

Yeah. That makes sense. I mean, I I think you also mentioned that you're investing something like $550,000,000 of cost savings into AI and platform on that on that same quarterly call. Where are you investing it, or where where are you putting the brunt of that, both capital and effort?

Speaker 1 | 23:39 - 24:03
Yeah. So we and and the amount we're investing is actually higher. That's not correct. There are lots of areas of investment. We talked to them on the call. That's not what's important is the idea that but you've got savings. You've got money.

I'm like, that doesn't seem like the right thing.

Right?

You have the marketplace built. Are there other aspects of your business that you view as especially durable going into this era?
